title: Nonlinear Photonic Tripartite Phase
authors: Xiangrui Hou, Fangyu Wang, Zhaoxin Wu, Shuming Zhang, Shan-Zhong Li, Lei Ying, Haiqing Lin, Baile Zhang, Zhi Li, Shi-Liang Zhu, Zhaoju Yang
abstract: Anderson localization is usually understood as a transition between extended and localized phases, with criticality confined to a single mobility edge. Recent advances predict that quasiperiodic systems can instead host a finite critical window bounded by mobility edges, in which localized, critical and extended states coexist. Yet both the experimental realization of this regime and whether interactions can provide controlled access to it remain unknown. Here, we realize such a tripartite phase in a nonlinear quasiperiodic photonic lattice and show that Kerr nonlinearity, acting as an effective interaction, enables state-selective access to the critical window. By tracking wavepacket dynamics, we distinguish localized, critical and extended transport regimes and uncover a state-selective response: rather than simply reinforcing localization through self-trapping, weak nonlinearity drives low-energy localized states into the critical window, whereas stronger nonlinearity restores localization. By contrast, critical, extended and high-energy localized states evolve monotonically towards self-trapped behaviour. Our results reveal a state-selective mechanism by which interactions provide controlled access to a pre-existing critical window in quasiperiodic systems.
summary: This paper experimentally demonstrates a tripartite phase in a nonlinear quasiperiodic photonic lattice where localized, critical, and extended states coexist. It reveals that nonlinearity acts selectively, capable of driving low-energy localized states into a critical window before eventually inducing self-trapping. This discovery offers a mechanism for using interactions to control state access in quasiperiodic landscapes.
main_problem: The study addresses the lack of experimental realization of a tripartite phase (coexisting localized, critical, and extended states) in quasiperiodic systems and investigates whether nonlinearity can provide controlled access to the critical window.
main_result: The researchers experimentally realize a tripartite phase in a nonlinear photonic lattice and discover a state-selective mechanism where weak nonlinearity 'thaws' low-energy localized states into the critical window, while stronger nonlinearity leads to self-trapping.
method: The study uses femtosecond-laser-written waveguide arrays to implement a quasiperiodic diamond lattice, employing site-selective excitation and tracking wavepacket dynamics and fractal dimension evolution.
model_or_system: A nonlinear quasiperiodic diamond photonic lattice implemented via waveguide arrays, governed by the discrete nonlinear Schrodinger equation with a quasiperiodic potential applied to one sublattice.
key_observables: Fractal Dimension (FD), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), wavepacket spatial intensity evolution, and energy-dependent transport regimes.
important_parameters: Disorder strength (lambda), nonlinearity strength (gP/J), and energy levels (low-energy vs. high-energy localized states).
main_assumptions: The paraxial approximation is used to map spatial propagation in waveguides to temporal evolution in quantum systems.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the waveguide array schematic and excitation sites; Fig 2 visualizes spatial intensity evolution; Fig 3 displays the fractal dimension as a function of nonlinearity and its dynamical evolution; Fig 4 shows the monotonic drive of certain states into the soliton regime.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental gap, describes the photonic lattice platform and Hamiltonian, presents the realization of the tripartite phase in the linear regime, explores the state-selective response to Kerr nonlinearity, and concludes with the underlying mechanism of spectral flow.
why_interesting: It provides a rare experimental demonstration of a tripartite phase and shows how nonlinear interactions can be used as a dynamical tool to navigate between different localization regimes, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and controlled transport in disordered systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental realization of a tripartite phase in a quasiperiodic diamond lattice. a, Schematic of the photonic waveguide array, where the quasiperiodic potential applied to sublattice A is depicted by the purple color gradient. The superimposed curve illustrates the on-site potential energy distribution. The red circles mark the selective excitation sites targeting the low-energy localized (A12), critical (C11), extended (C12), and high-energy localized (A13) states. b, Phase diagram for a system size of L = 987, with black dashed lines indicating mobility edges. c, Spectrum of the fractal dimension FD. The dependence of FD on energy E is plotted for a fixed disorder strength λ =...

FIG. 2. Dynamical signatures of the tripartite phase. a, Evolution of the fractal dimension FD as a function of propagation distance z. The experimental measurements (dots) are consistent with the numerical simulations (solid lines). b, Spatial intensity distributions measured at propagation lengths of z =2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 cm. The columns illustrate three distinct transport regimes: the localized state remains spatially confined (1st and 4th column), the critical state exhibits anomalous expansion (2nd column), and the extended state undergoes ballistic spreading (3rd column). Lattice parameters: system size L = 21, coupling strength J = t = 0.5 cm−1, and disorder strength λ = 1.3J.

FIG. 3. Interaction response in the tripartite phase. a, The time-averaged fractal dimension FD as a function of interaction strength, for the low-energy (L. E.) localized (A12), critical (C11), extended (C12), and high-energy (H. E.) localized (A13) states. The value is integrated over the propagation window z ∈[20, 50]cm. b, Dynamical evolution of the FD for the three interaction strengths. Experimental measurements (dots) show excellent agreement with numerical simulations (solid lines), confirming the non-monotonic transition. All parameters are consistent with Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. State-selective nonlinear transport and re-entrant localization. Numerical (a-d) and experimental (e-h) spatial intensity distributions for the low-energy localized state (a, e), critical state (b, f), extended state (c, g), and high-energy localized state (d, h). Each panel displays wavepacket profiles stacked vertically in ascending order of input power gP/J. A striking contrast is observed: the low-energy localized state (a, e) exhibits a unique non-monotonic anomalous transition (localization →critical transport →soliton), whereas the critical, extended, and high-energy localized states (b-d, f-h) undergo monotonic self-trapping, directly collapsing into the soliton regime.
